questions.question_text: Under AB 1482, what is the maximum annual rent increase a landlord may charge for a covered unit?
questions.correct_answer: 5% plus local CPI, not to exceed 10%
distractors: 5% flat rate statewide
10% flat rate statewide
3% plus local CPI, not to exceed 8%
questions.explanation: AB 1482 (Tenant Protection Act) caps annual rent increases at 5% plus local CPI, with a maximum combined cap of 10%. This applies to covered residential rentals throughout California.

questions.question_text: A landlord owns a single-family home and rents it to a tenant, but never served the AB 1482 exemption notice. The tenant has lived there for 18 months. The landlord wants to terminate the tenancy for no specific reason. Under AB 1482, the landlord:
questions.correct_answer: Must provide just cause for termination after 12 months
distractors: May terminate at any time with 60 days' written notice
Cannot terminate until the fixed-term lease expires
May terminate freely because single-family homes are exempt from AB 1482
questions.explanation: After 12 months of continuous occupancy, tenants in covered units are entitled to just cause eviction protection under AB 1482. Single-family homes can be exempt, but only if the landlord has properly served the tenant with a required exemption notice. Without that notice, just cause applies.

questions.question_text: A landlord terminates a long-term tenant's tenancy for no-fault reasons (owner move-in). What must the landlord provide?
questions.correct_answer: 60 days' notice and 1 month's relocation assistance
distractors: 30 days' notice only
90 days' notice and 2 months' relocation assistance
30 days' notice; relocation assistance is optional
questions.explanation: Under AB 1482, no-fault terminations require the landlord to provide 60 days' written notice and pay the tenant one month's rent as relocation assistance.

questions.question_text: A building was constructed 10 years ago. Is it likely covered by AB 1482 rent caps?
questions.correct_answer: No, buildings constructed within the last 15 years are exempt
distractors: Yes, all residential rentals built after 1995 are covered
Yes, unless it is a single-family home with the proper notice
No, only buildings 20+ years old fall under AB 1482
questions.explanation: AB 1482 exempts residential units that were first issued a certificate of occupancy within the last 15 years from the date of the rent increase. A 10-year-old building falls within this window and is exempt from the rent cap.

questions.question_text: Under the Costa-Hawkins Act, which types of properties are exempt from local rent control ordinances?
questions.correct_answer: Single-family homes and units first occupied after 1995
distractors: Only government-subsidized housing developments
Properties with fewer than 10 units in any city
All residential units built before 1980
questions.explanation: Costa-Hawkins limits local rent control by exempting single-family homes, condominiums, and units first occupied after February 1, 1995. Local cities cannot apply their rent control ordinances to these property types.

questions.question_text: Under AB 12 (effective July 2024), a landlord of a standard rental unit may collect a security deposit of no more than:
distractors: 2 months' rent
3 months' rent
6 weeks' rent
questions.explanation: AB 12, effective July 1, 2024, reduced the maximum security deposit for most residential rentals to 1 month's rent — down from the prior limits of 2 months' rent for unfurnished units and 3 months' for furnished units.

questions.question_text: A small landlord owns 2 duplexes (4 total units) and charges a new tenant a security deposit of 2 months' rent. This is:
questions.correct_answer: Permitted under the small landlord exception allowing up to 2 months
distractors: Prohibited; all landlords are limited to 1 month under AB 12
Permitted only if the tenant agrees in writing
Prohibited unless the unit is furnished
questions.explanation: AB 12 includes a small landlord exception: landlords who own no more than 2 properties containing a total of no more than 4 units may still charge up to 2 months' rent as a security deposit.

questions.question_text: A tenant moves out on June 1. The landlord discovers damage and uses $800 of the deposit for repairs. When must the landlord return the remaining deposit and what is required?
questions.correct_answer: Within 21 days with an itemized statement of deductions
distractors: Within 30 days with a general statement of deductions
Within 14 days with receipts for all repairs
Within 21 days with no itemization required for amounts under $1,500
questions.explanation: California Civil Code requires landlords to return the security deposit (or remainder) within 21 days of the tenant vacating, accompanied by an itemized written statement of any deductions.

questions.question_text: A tenant reports a cockroach infestation to the landlord. The landlord ignores the complaint for three months. The tenant pays for professional extermination and deducts the cost from rent. Under CC §1941, the tenant's action is:
questions.correct_answer: Permitted under the repair and deduct remedy up to one month's rent
distractors: Prohibited; tenants may never withhold rent in California
Permitted only if the tenant gave 60 days' written notice first
Prohibited because pest control is the tenant's responsibility
questions.explanation: Civil Code §1941 sets the landlord's habitability standards; the repair-and-deduct remedy itself comes from §1942. After reasonable notice goes unaddressed, the tenant may repair and deduct up to one month's rent, usable at most twice in any 12-month period.

questions.question_text: A tenant complains to the city about substandard conditions in October. The landlord serves an eviction notice in February. The landlord claims unrelated cause. Under California law:
questions.correct_answer: Retaliation is presumed if eviction occurs within 180 days of the protected complaint
distractors: The landlord's claim is presumed valid because 4 months have passed
The tenant must prove retaliatory intent by clear and convincing evidence
There is no statutory presumption of retaliation in California
questions.explanation: California creates a rebuttable presumption of retaliatory eviction when the landlord takes adverse action within 180 days of the tenant's exercise of a protected right, such as reporting habitability violations to a government agency.

questions.question_text: A property manager collects rents and negotiates leases on behalf of a building owner in exchange for a monthly fee. Does this require a real estate license?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, managing property for compensation requires a real estate license
distractors: No, property management is exempt from licensing requirements
No, if the manager is a W-2 employee of the property owner
Yes, but only for buildings with more than 4 units
questions.explanation: California requires a real estate license for anyone who manages property for others for compensation. This includes collecting rents and negotiating leases. The employee exception applies only to employees who manage the employer's own property.

questions.question_text: Under the Mobilehome Residency Law (Civil Code §798), what is the minimum lease term a mobilehome park owner must offer a resident?
distractors: Month-to-month only
6 months
24 months
questions.explanation: The Mobilehome Residency Law (CC §798) requires mobilehome park owners to offer residents a written lease of at least 12 months. Residents may choose a shorter term, but the offer of a 12-month lease must be made.

questions.question_text: A tenant in a mobilehome park has lived there for 5 years. The park owner wants to evict the tenant to redevelop the land. Under Civil Code §798, the owner:
questions.correct_answer: Must have just cause to evict a long-term resident
distractors: May evict without cause at any time with 60 days' notice
May only evict if the tenant has violated park rules
Must offer the tenant an equivalent site within the park
questions.explanation: The Mobilehome Residency Law provides just cause eviction protection for mobilehome park residents. The park owner cannot simply evict a long-term resident to redevelop without meeting statutory just cause requirements.

questions.question_text: A property management company wants to use an unlicensed employee to show vacant apartments to prospective tenants. This is:
questions.correct_answer: Permitted — unlicensed employees may show residential apartments under supervision
distractors: Not permitted because showing property always requires a real estate license
Permitted only if the employee holds a property management certificate
Permitted for commercial properties but not residential
questions.explanation: B&P Code §10131.01 exempts unlicensed employees of a landlord or property management broker from licensing for limited residential apartment activities: showing units, providing factual information from written statements, handing out and accepting applications, and accepting deposits or rent — under the broker's supervision and without negotiating lease terms. Contrast sales: showing a home listed for sale still requires a license.

questions.question_text: A tenant wants to withhold all rent until the landlord repairs a broken heating system in January. The landlord has ignored notices for 6 weeks. Which California remedy allows this?
questions.correct_answer: The rent withholding remedy under CC §1941 for uninhabitable conditions
distractors: The tenant has no right to withhold rent under any circumstances
The constructive eviction remedy requiring the tenant to vacate first
The repair-and-deduct remedy, limited to one month's rent
questions.explanation: California allows tenants to withhold rent when a landlord fails to maintain habitable conditions under CC §1941. A broken heating system in winter renders a unit uninhabitable. Rent withholding is a separate remedy from repair-and-deduct.
